name: Argentina
tactical_document:
# Argentina — Tactical Profile

## Identity & Philosophy
Reigning World Cup champions (2022) and back-to-back Copa América winners (2021, 2024), Argentina under Lionel Scaloni are the most complete side of their generation: pragmatic but technical, comfortable in possession yet ruthless on the break. Scaloni's philosophy fuses Bielsa-era verticality with Simeone-era defensive grit, all built around giving Lionel Messi total freedom in the final third. The knockout run has hardened the team into comeback specialists — three straight knockout wins from losing positions — and the battle-tested **4-4-2** with a double pivot returns for the final after Scaloni's 4-3-3 semifinal experiment was rescued from the bench.

**Group stage (Group J): won it.** Opened against Algeria, six early points, closed with a 2-0 win over Austria. Messi seized the all-time World Cup scoring record along the way.

**Round of 32 — Argentina 3-2 Cabo Verde (AET, Fri 3 Jul, Miami).** The near-death experience: only a 111th-minute own goal spared the champions the biggest upset in tournament history. 120 draining minutes in the Miami heat.

**Round of 16 — Argentina 3-2 Egypt (Tue 7 Jul, Atlanta).** From 0-2 down with ten minutes of normal time left: Romero's header, Messi's smashed 83rd-minute equaliser, and Enzo Fernández's stoppage-time winning header.

**Quarter-final — Argentina 3-1 Switzerland (AET, Sat 11 Jul, Kansas City).** Mac Allister headed in Messi's corner; Ndoye levelled; Embolo's second yellow left the Swiss with ten; Álvarez broke his drought with the 112th-minute winner and substitute Lautaro Martínez sealed it at 120+1.

**Semi-final — Argentina 2-1 England (Wed 15 Jul, Mercedes-Benz Stadium, Atlanta).** Another comeback, the third of the knockouts. Scaloni started a 4-3-3 — De Paul benched, Giuliano Simeone on the right of the front three — and it misfired: Anthony Gordon volleyed England ahead on 55' from Morgan Rogers' long cross, and Molina was repeatedly stretched by Gordon and Djed Spence. The bench won it. De Paul came on and changed the game (two key passes, one big chance created); **Enzo Fernández bent a left-footed equaliser from outside the box on 85' off Messi's pass**, and **Lautaro Martínez headed the 90+2' winner from Messi's cross** — "I dreamed it, I swear," he said. Messi, at 39, assisted both goals. Mac Allister also headed against the post. No Argentine was sent off and no suspension carries into the final; no new injuries were reported.

**FINAL — Spain (Sun 19 Jul, MetLife Stadium, East Rutherford, 3pm ET).** Expected XI restores the knockout-proven 4-4-2: **Emi Martínez; Molina, Romero, Lisandro Martínez, Tagliafico; De Paul, Paredes, Enzo Fernández, Mac Allister; Messi, Álvarez.** De Paul returns for Simeone (the consensus across previews); Scaloni is reported to stick with the Messi-Álvarez partnership and keep Lautaro as the super-sub hammer despite his winner; Montiel is pushing Molina at right-back after the Gordon/Spence torment, but with no injury or suspension involved the incumbent is retained here. Availability is perfect: nobody is injured, nobody is suspended.

## Formation
- Shape: **4-4-2** (flat two banks out of possession; in possession Paredes drops into a 4-1-3-2 diamond with Enzo advancing and Messi roaming off Álvarez)
- Role mapping (roster order in `argentina.yaml`):
  - index 0: GK — **Emiliano Martínez** (#23) — elite shot-stopper, vocal organizer, mentality monster on penalties. Stays in his box and dominates aerially; if the final reaches twelve yards he is Argentina's decisive edge, the 2022 shootout hero.
  - index 1: LB — **Nicolás Tagliafico** (#3) — disciplined 2022-final veteran. His flank is the red zone of this final: **Lamine Yamal attacks it**, cutting in off Spain's right with Porro overlapping outside. Overlaps are rare; Mac Allister doubles back with him all night.
  - index 2: LCB — **Lisandro Martínez** (#6) — combative, intelligent left-footed ball-playing CB; strong in the tackle, steps out to intercept, distributes crisply. Scored vs Cabo Verde — a set-piece threat too. Covers when Romero steps to Oyarzabal's drop-ins.
  - index 3: RCB — **Cristian Romero** (#13) — front-foot, aggressive stepper; tracks Oyarzabal when the Spain #9 drops short to link, but never so far that Olmo's late run finds the vacated space.
  - index 4: RB — **Nahuel Molina** (#26) — provides all the width on the right because Messi drifts inside. Had a hard semifinal against Gordon and Spence (Montiel is breathing down his neck), but keeps the shirt; against Spain his side faces Baena drifting inside with Cucurella overlapping — he must pass Baena on and take the overlap.
  - index 5: LM — **Alexis Mac Allister** (#20) — left-sided midfielder who tucks inside; scored the opener vs Switzerland and headed the post vs England. Prime second-phase set-piece threat, and Tagliafico's doubling partner on **Yamal** — the single most important defensive assignment of the final.
  - index 6: LCM — **Enzo Fernández** (#24) — the man of the semifinal: the bending 85th-minute equaliser from outside the box (his second late knockout goal after the Egypt winner). Box-to-box playmaker who advances into the left half-space when Paredes drops; long diagonals, split passes, late arrivals. His duel with Rodri's pivot decides who owns the middle.
  - index 7: RCM/regista — **Leandro Paredes** (#5) — the deep metronome; dictates tempo with long switches, screens the back four, and is Argentina's most reliable penalty taker. Against Spain his screening of the **Olmo pocket** between the lines is the single most important defensive job on the pitch. Card-prone — a red is the only discipline that can hurt now.
  - index 8: RM — **Rodrigo De Paul** (#7) — restored to the XI after the bench cameo that turned the semifinal (two key passes, one big chance). Messi's bodyguard, box-to-box engine, all-action presser; shuttles to cover Molina's overlaps and tracks Fabián Ruiz's late arrivals from deep.
  - index 9: ST — **Julián Álvarez** (#9) — keeps the shirt over Lautaro for mobility and pressing; quiet vs England but his channel-running is the weapon built for Spain's **high line** — the space behind Laporte and Cubarsí is the biggest prize on the pitch. Leads the press on Rodri's supply.
  - index 10: CF/free role — **Lionel Messi** (#10) — floats off Álvarez, drifting right, between the lines, wherever the game is. No defensive responsibility. 8 goals plus both semifinal assists; the all-time World Cup top scorer, playing what is surely his last World Cup match. At 39, after three brutal knockout nights, every sprint is budgeted. In-match penalties remain Paredes' (Messi has missed two this tournament).

## Style of Play
### Build-up
Argentina will NOT win the possession battle — Spain average ~65% and will be allowed to keep sterile ball. When Argentina do build: Emi Martínez splits the CBs, Paredes drops between them to form a 3+1, fullbacks push to mid-height only (Spain's counter-press is elite — the 2-3-5 rest-defence behind it less so). Progression runs through Paredes' long switches and Enzo stepping into the left half-space. Spain press high on triggers (back-pass, heavy touch) with Oyarzabal curving onto Paredes and Olmo jumping the pivot — the answer is the third-man bounce (CB to De Paul, first-time into Messi's feet) and above all the **early ball over the top for Álvarez**: Spain's line is the highest Argentina have faced, and one flighted pass from Paredes or Enzo beats sixty of their passes.

### Pressing
**Mid-block** primarily, compact and patient — chasing Spain's U-shape circulation is how legs die. Selective high-press triggers only: a back-pass to Unai Simón, or a square CB-to-CB pass in flight. Álvarez leads the press — curving runs to shadow **Rodri**, Spain's metronome; if Rodri receives clean, the press has already failed. Messi does NOT press; he hovers between the lines, conserving energy for the transition. De Paul and Mac Allister jump into midfield duels only when the ball is trapped on a touchline. Rest-defence discipline is paramount: Paredes plus both CBs stay anchored at all times.

### Defensive shape
A genuine flat **4-4-2**: Mac Allister and De Paul tuck into the midfield four alongside Enzo and Paredes; Messi walks alongside Álvarez up top. The three Spain problems to solve: (1) **Yamal cutting in from the right** — Tagliafico shows him outside, Mac Allister doubles inside, and the near-side CB guards the curler's arc to the far post; (2) **Olmo between the lines** — Paredes' pocket, never a CB stepping out; (3) **the fullback overlaps** — when Yamal comes inside to Mac Allister, Tagliafico takes Porro's overlap; on the other flank Molina and De Paul split Baena-inside/Cucurella-outside. Keep the block at mid-height: sitting on the six-yard box invites Spain's cutback machine (Oyarzabal at the spot, Olmo and Fabián arriving late).

### Wide play
Asymmetric: **right** = Molina's overlap with De Paul covering, feeding the Messi-Molina-De Paul triangle where most chances are manufactured — and where Spain are softest, behind Cucurella when he has pushed on; **left** = Tagliafico stays home almost entirely — his flank belongs to Yamal — with Mac Allister drifting inside and Enzo underlapping only on secured possession. Fatigue caveat: three straight comeback knockouts means fullbacks pick their sprints — controlled bursts, not waves.

### Final third
Transition is the plan: Argentina's best chances will come in the 10 seconds after winning the ball, before Spain's counter-press lands. First look is always vertical — Messi between the lines or Álvarez over the top against the high line. Patterns: Messi dropping to slip Álvarez in behind; Messi-to-Molina cut-back from the right byline; Enzo's late arrival or long-range strike (the England equaliser — shoot from range when Spain's block re-sets); Mac Allister attacking the back post. If it opens up late, **Lautaro Martínez off the bench has scored decisive late goals in two straight knockouts** (120+1 vs Switzerland, 90+2 vs England) — the fresh-legs hammer is proven.

## Set Pieces
- Attacking corners: Messi delivers from the right (in-swinger) — his corner made Mac Allister's opener vs Switzerland — Mac Allister from the left (in-swinger). Primary aerial targets: Romero, Lisandro Martínez, Enzo Fernández arriving late (the Egypt winner), Mac Allister at the back post. Paredes loiters at the edge for rebounds. Spain defend zonally with Rodri patrolling the edge — the edge-of-box second ball is where Enzo's strike from the semifinal lives.
- Defending corners: **hybrid** — three zonal markers on the six-yard line, four man-markers, two short-corner blockers. Romero attacks the first ball. Spain's in-swingers from Baena (left) and Yamal (right) target Laporte, Cubarsí and Olmo. Keep Álvarez high as the counter outlet.
- Free kicks: Messi takes direct from any zone within 30 yards — non-negotiable. Paredes and Enzo deliver wide free kicks. Discipline near the box is critical both ways: Spain won their semifinal penalty off a Yamal-drawn foul — do NOT dangle a leg at him in the area.
- Penalties (in match): **Paredes** primary — Messi has missed two this tournament and has handed over spot-kick duty. **Mac Allister** secondary, **Álvarez** tertiary.
- **Penalty-shootout order:**
  1. **Paredes** — ice-cold, scored in the 2022 final shootout (penalty 16).
  2. **Messi** — still steps up first-five in a shootout despite the in-match misses (penalty 14).
  3. **Mac Allister** — composed, high technique (penalty 15).
  4. **Álvarez** — penalty 15.
  5. **Enzo Fernández** — penalty 15.
  - Behind them all: **Emi Martínez** (#23), the 2022 shootout hero — against Unai Simón, himself a shootout winner for Spain, Argentina still hold the edge from twelve yards.

## decide() Decision Priorities
Concrete rules the LLM should encode:
1. **If my `player_id` ends with `_10` (Messi, free role) and team_phase == "defending":** Hold or slow-walk toward the halfway line — do NOT track back. Conserve stamina; at 39, in his last World Cup match, every sprint is budgeted for the transition.
2. **If my `player_id` ends with `_10` (Messi) and I have the ball between the lines:** Prefer the killer Pass — ST `_9` (Álvarez) running in behind Spain's high line, or RB `_4` (Molina) overlapping right into the space Cucurella vacates. Shoot when within 22 units with a lane (shoot 19); do NOT hold the ball under Spain's 6-second counter-press — release and go again.
3. **If my `role == "GK"` (player_id `_0`, Emi Martínez):** Stay home — sweep only up to 20 units out of the box when a through-ball is certain to be reached first. Distribute short to `_2`/`_3` unless pressed, then long toward `_9`'s channel run over Spain's high press.
4. **If my `player_id` ends with `_7` (Paredes, regista) and team_phase == "attacking":** Drop between/behind the CBs to form the 3+1; recycle with safe forward passes and hit the early diagonal over the top for `_9` when Spain's line is high — one flighted ball beats sixty of theirs. Never carry into traffic — Spain's counter-press is the best in the tournament. If no forward option, pass square to a CB.
5. **If my `player_id` ends with `_4` (RB, Molina) and team_phase == "attacking" and the ball is central or left:** Advance high and wide right — the overlap is automatic because `_10` drifts inside, and Spain's left is their softer defensive flank. But if the team just lost the ball, sprint back immediately — Yamal counters into the fullback zones.
6. **If my `player_id` ends with `_8` (RM, De Paul) and the ball-carrier's `player_id` ends with `_10` (Messi):** Position 8-12 units behind and to the carrier's right — cover-shadow for the counter-press the moment `_10` loses it; Spain's transition through Olmo and Fabián starts on exactly those turnovers.
7. **If my `player_id` ends with `_6` (LCM, Enzo) and team_phase == "attacking":** If the ball is wide, make the late run into the box toward the far post (the Egypt winner); if Spain's block has re-set and I receive at the edge, Shoot from range (shoot 15 — the England equaliser). Otherwise offer the half-space link and hit split passes (pass 18).
8. **If my `player_id` ends with `_5` (LM, Mac Allister) and team_phase == "defending":** Tuck inside and double on Spain's right winger (`Spain _10`, Yamal) with `_1` (Tagliafico) — show him outside, never let the left-foot cut inside come free; when possession is won, release `_9` or find `_10` between the lines before Spain's block re-sets. On attacking corners from the right, attack the back post.
9. **If my `player_id` ends with `_9` (ST, Álvarez) and team_phase == "defending":** Stay high as the front two with `_10` and cover-shadow Spain's deepest midfielder (Rodri, `Spain _6`) — deny him clean receptions; do NOT drop into midfield. On transition, run in behind IMMEDIATELY — Spain's line is the highest Argentina have faced and the space behind it is the whole game plan.
10. **If my `role == "FWD"` or `role == "MID"` and Argentina just lost the ball in the opposition third:** Counter-press — Tackle the nearest opponent within 6 units for 5 seconds; deny the first pass out before Spain's circulation restarts. Exception: `_10` does not counter-press.
11. **If team is leading by 1+ goals and minute > 75:** Drop into the compact mid-block, protect the box, and break only with numbers-up — do NOT chase Spain's possession or press high; `_7` (Paredes) kills tempo on every restart, `_8`/`_5` hold the ball in the corners when it comes. Never foul near the box — Spain scored their semifinal opener from a drawn penalty.
12. **Penalties:** in-match penalty goes to `_7` (Paredes), NOT `_10` — Messi has missed two this tournament. Direct free kicks within 30 units remain `_10`'s (Messi).

## Key Player Notes
- **Messi (#10, idx 10):** Free role off the striker. No defensive responsibility — walking is a tactical instruction. 8 goals, all-time World Cup top scorer, and both semifinal assists (the pass for Enzo's equaliser, the cross for Lautaro's winner) in what is surely his final World Cup match — chasing a second straight title at 39. Every direct free kick is his; in-match spot kicks belong to Paredes; Messi still takes in a shootout.
- **Enzo Fernández (#24, idx 6):** The semifinal hero — the bending 85th-minute equaliser from outside the box, his second decisive late knockout goal (Egypt winner). Advanced 8 with Paredes behind him dictating; his duel with Rodri and Fabián for the middle is the battle of the final.
- **Álvarez (#9, idx 9):** Keeps the shirt over Lautaro for mobility and pressing. Quiet vs England, but he is the player Spain's high line fears most — channel runs in behind Laporte/Cubarsí are the template, and he shadows Rodri out of possession.
- **Paredes (#5, idx 7):** Regista who controls tempo and screens the Olmo pocket — the most important defensive job on the pitch. His early diagonals over Spain's line are the fastest route to goal. Argentina's best penalty taker and first in the shootout order. Card-prone; only a red hurts now.
- **De Paul (#7, idx 8):** Restored to the XI after his semifinal cameo turned the tie (two key passes, one big chance created). Messi's bodyguard on the right of the midfield four; counter-presses Messi's losses and tracks Fabián's late arrivals.
- **Mac Allister (#20, idx 5):** Headed the post vs England, scored the opener vs Switzerland. Left midfield, tucks inside; gets the tournament's hardest defensive brief — doubling Yamal with Tagliafico — plus late runs, second balls, left-side corners and the second penalty.
- **Lisandro Martínez (#6, idx 2):** Left-footed LCB; combative, reads the game, crisp distribution; scored vs Cabo Verde. Guards the far-post arc when Yamal shapes to curl.
- **Romero (#13, idx 3):** Aggressive RCB; tracks Oyarzabal's drop-ins but must resist being pulled out — Olmo's late runs punish the vacated space. His header ignited the Egypt comeback.
- **Tagliafico (#3, idx 1):** Disciplined 2022-final veteran with the assignment of his life: Lamine Yamal, one-v-one plus Porro's overlap. Shows him outside, trusts the double from Mac Allister, and does not dangle a leg in the box — Yamal drew the penalty that beat France.
- **Molina (#26, idx 4):** Keeps the RB shirt despite a rough night against Gordon and Spence (Montiel pushed hard in previews — tactical debate, not injury). Supplies all the right-side width; attacks the space behind Cucurella.
- **Emi Martínez (#23, idx 0):** Elite shot-stopper and the 2022 shootout ace — if the final goes to penalties, he is the difference even against Simón, who set a World Cup record with 6 clean sheets this tournament.
- **Bench:** **Lautaro Martínez (#22)** is the tournament's super-sub — decisive late goals in two straight knockouts (120+1 vs Switzerland, the 90+2 semifinal winner vs England: "I dreamed it, I swear"). He stays the closer unless Scaloni springs a surprise. **Giuliano Simeone** started the semifinal right wing and drops out for De Paul; **Montiel** is first RB alternative and a 2022 shootout scorer; Nico González, Otamendi and Lo Celso are the late-game locks; Almada and José López give legs.

## Tournament Mindset
**THE FINAL — Sunday 19 July, MetLife Stadium, one win from back-to-back World Cups.** Argentina v Spain: the reigning world champions against the reigning European champions, the tournament's best comeback team against its best defence. Argentina have trailed in every knockout round and won all four; Spain have conceded once in seven games, beat France 2-0 in their semifinal (Oyarzabal's 22nd-minute penalty after Yamal drew the foul, Porro's 58th-minute give-and-go finish), and Unai Simón holds a World Cup-record 6 clean sheets. This is Spain's first final since 2010; Argentina are chasing a fourth star and the first repeat title since Brazil 1962. And it is Lionel Messi's last World Cup match — 8 goals, both semifinal assists, the story of the sport closing at 39.

Selection reasoning: the base is the semifinal-winning core, adjusted the way every credible preview expects — **De Paul returns for Simeone** (the 4-3-3 misfired; De Paul changed the game off the bench), restoring the knockout-proven 4-4-2; **Álvarez keeps the shirt over Lautaro**, whose value is precisely the fresh-legged final half-hour (late winners in two straight rounds); **Molina is retained over Montiel** — the RB swap floated in one preview is tactical speculation, not injury or suspension, and continuity wins three days before a final. Nobody is injured, nobody is suspended: no Argentine was booked out of the semifinal and the card slate was wiped after the quarter-finals.

The shape of the game is known in advance: Spain will have the ball — 65% of it — and Argentina must be comfortable without it, as they were for stretches against England. The block stays compact at mid-height, Paredes strangles the Olmo pocket, Álvarez shadows Rodri, and Tagliafico and Mac Allister spend the evening doubling Yamal. The prize is behind Spain's high line: one early diagonal from Paredes or Enzo onto Álvarez's run beats an hour of circulation, and Messi between the lines in transition remains the tournament's most dangerous single event. Do not chase, do not foul near the box (Yamal manufactures penalties), do not trade possession sequences with the best keep-ball team on earth. Hold Lautaro — the man who dreams winners — for the final act, and if it reaches twelve yards, Argentina carry Paredes, Messi and Emiliano Martínez, the 2022 shootout hands. Champions defend their crown the way they won it: suffering first, striking once, and never, ever believing they are beaten.
